speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] two minutes. [INAUDIBLE] to go to the wedding with my friends. They used to go ahead of me, but [INAUDIBLE]. I wouldn't make it. So this was [INAUDIBLE] who got married? [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] Have you seen the shoes? There was a lady that I interviewed like 12 weeks ago. She had [INAUDIBLE]. She brought out a picture of herself and her mother. And it just was like two months after the war, and she went to [INAUDIBLE] Budapest [INAUDIBLE], and they had very nice clothes. Their clothes, that could have been today picture-- the shoes, the style of--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the 9th of October, 1995. I am Patricia Stein, here to interview Henry Kinsley, born Kunstlinger, in Delray Beach, Florida, United States. The interview is in English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Today's the 9th of October, 1995. I am Patricia Stein, here to interview Henry Kinsley, born Kunstlinger. The interview is in Delray Beach, Florida, United States. Interview  is in English. This is tape one. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Good morning, Mr. Kinsley. Could you give me your name, your name at birth, if you had a nickname, your birth date, and the city and country where you were born, and how old you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name is Henry Kinsley, used to be Joachim Kunstlinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Krakow, Poland, November 11, 1915. Oh, that's good, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Kunstlinger?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-- two dots, umlaut they're called-- N-S-T-L-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, I'll be in November, 80.

speaker: interviewer
text: You grew up, you said, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Krakow. Went to school in Krakow, elementary, high school. And then I, after graduation, I went to Warsaw and studied polytechnic until the war broke out in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a quarter-million, 250,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish people lived in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I don't know. There were quite a few hundred-- 80,000, 100,000. And they mostly were merchants, storekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they concentrated in one area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Mostly, there was a-- I wouldn't call this a ghetto. They'll call this [NON-ENGLISH]. Which was a section where mostly they concentrate. But there was [INAUDIBLE], dependent on the financial situation. If he were better off, he moved from this [NON-ENGLISH] and went all over Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jewish people mostly well-off, or what was their financial situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing, same thing. I mean, we were better off in Krakow than in the small towns. And there were organizations who helped them, you know. And there are famous people we got over there, Dr. Thon, which was later elected to the Polish parliament.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Dr. Thon, T-H-O-N. I even graduated from Hebrew in high school, in front of was the chairman. And we used to have a temple over there, which he was in charge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he died before the war, a good speaker, and a nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, I got beside me three brothers. That's the only [INAUDIBLE]-- no girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Solomon, and David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kunstlinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Joseph Kunstlinger. My mother maiden name was Wieser W-I-E-- oh, the other way-- I-E-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eleanor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents earn their living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a partner in so-called municipal bank, which supported farmers all over Poland with loans. And that's the way earn their living, and that was very good off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your brothers help your father in the bank?

speaker: interviewee
text: My elder brother helped them. Then I helped them a little bit. I got time from school. And the younger brother, he was still in school. And the youngest was 13 only, so this went to school and studied. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about your education? Where did you go to school? What's your schools' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in Krakow Podgorze. This was a suburb of Krakow, which the school was [INAUDIBLE] in the Polish, [POLISH] Tadeusz Kosciuszko. Tadeusz Kosciuszko was a Polish hero who would emigrate later to America. I think he took part in-- he did a bridge in New York. 'Kosh-u-as-ko' they called it. You know, they can't pronounce it Kosciuszko. I finished elementary school. Then I graduate after some test to the gymnasium, they called it, which is a high school, which got, besides Polish studies, and I think six or seven hours a week in Hebrew. And I graduated there eight years from both Polish study and Hebrew. Then I went to Warsaw, where I study polytechnic. And the name of the school is Politechnika Warszawska. There were two in Poland. One was in  Warsaw. The other was in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew how to draw a little bit. And the only-- I got connections, let's put it this way, to get in. Because it was a close quarter. So I got a little push from a very famous painter in Poland. His name was [? Juliusz ?] [? Kossak. ?] And I drew him my garden. Not because of my talent or something, just because I know him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had you displayed your painting ability before, your drawing ability?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did only for friends or something. I didn't pursue as a trader or nothing, just like today, a hobby. It's a hobby. That's all. Keep my time, my drawing. And my friend move in an apartment. Hey, Henry, make me a picture. I made him a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: In school, there was a-- I remember-- an anniversary of our principal. So each class gave him something. So what happened, they came to me. Hey, why don't you make a portrait of the principal? I made a portrait of the principal. And that's it. But never for money. Was anybody else in your family talented?

speaker: interviewee
text: Talented-- my father used to draw a lot of lettering in Polish and Hebrew. But he never-- you know, he's got a handwriting. Today, you pay where they're making invitation. They call this calligraphy. He got a handwriting, normal handwriting like this. So that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any Hebrew lettering, like to fix a Torah or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Torah, remember [INAUDIBLE] special guy who know how to do it, because he made it. Because I got a friend who went to school with me, very talented in Hebrew. He was very young. And he used to read Torah in the temple or shul in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: He found on a mistake in a Torah which was 30 years old. He emigrated to Israel. And he was in university in Jerusalem, but making new words, modern words, you know? His name was [INAUDIBLE], very talented. He died a few years ago, though. So there were special guys who write the Torah. We donated a Torah. I remember my mother, her brother was in the first war. And she said, if he comes alive, she's going to donate a Torah to the shul, what we call temple. And that's what she did. Was your family very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very, but religious, you know? Like I said, they keep the culture at home. But my father used to go every Saturday to shul. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with him? Did the kids go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got with him, you know, but he forced me. [LAUGHS] No, I had to go. As a kid we had to go. And later on, we got other interests. We couldn't handle it anymore. Yeah. So that's the story about the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What rituals did you observe? Did you do Friday nights, or did you observe Shabbat the whole Saturday?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were kids, so after my father came from shul, we got our meal dinner, Friday dinner. And after dinner Friday, we went to our grandfather which lived right across. All the kids, with my mother and my father, we stay over there with him. And there was a ceremony, where we went over there. We had to kiss to hand of my grandfather and grandmother, The same thing when we went to school. We went to my mother, kissed her hand, my father. This was normal. Today, you have to be French to do this. So no, it was very, very close family. And my parents tried to keep the tradition as much we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, while you were at school or growing up, did you come across any acts of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me, because I never looked like a Jew. I'm not kidding. I got some problems, because there was a situation after the war where I smuggle myself from Poland to Czechoslovakia. And somebody told me way to smugglers. They said, why don't you go? There's a place where the boys from Haganah bring in people to American zone. And I went over there. Took me three days. With my name, Joachim Kunstlinger, umlaut, took me three days to convince them I'm Jewish. I'm not kidding.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were young, was there any, like, the kids in your class or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The teachers wouldn't  teach you, or that kind of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the school, elementary school, were too young to understand this. In the high school, this was a Hebrew school. All teachers were Jewish. And we got no problem. On the street, you know, but I never had problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in Warsaw, when you were at the Polytechnic?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one situation over there. There were five Jews studying the same thing. Nobody knew I'm Jewish. And we were sitting one day and a bunch of, they called them [INAUDIBLE], came, and they start beating the five Jews, not me. But I couldn't take it, because there were three guys beating one guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, oh, no. So I start over there, help the guy, and we're on our way. They were following me, who I am, I mean, where I come defend a Jew? So I don't know how they found out. There I was, sitting once drawing in school over there. And I felt my instinct that somebody is behind me. I turn. He's got a razor blade in a cane and tried to hit me. I put my hand down and I got until today a cut from the things. That's the only way. Then, on next time, I remember when there were riots. Every year there was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the university, you know, the right wingers, got together and speeches, you know? And my father find out over there from newspaper. He called me up, say you better come home. So I go. I had to have a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pass so I can leave the town. I went to the university. And I find myself with about 500 people. One guy stays over there, tell us we go outside, and we go beat the Jews up. And I say with them, you know, yeah, yeah, you know? Because I find out they close the door. I couldn't get out. So I was staying with them. Guy want to embrace me. He coming at me, go in. So I said, sure, sure, we go in. By the way, go check if the door is open already. I went. The door is open, I never show up again. You know what I mean? But I was with them staying, you know, trying my best to-- but I never got problems. So after three years at the Polytechnic, what made you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. When the war broke out in September, we supposed to start another year in September, October. But the war broke out, and that's it. Finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happens? Did they say that as a Jew, could you go back to the school,or did you have to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no more school, because Warsaw was bombed. And Poland took two weeks, and the war is over in Poland. German occupied, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there at the time that the Germans occupied Poland or Warsaw, or were you still at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to-- there was a situation where we got an order from, because I was mobilized. If the war breaks out, I have to join the Polish army. So what happened? I got a mobilization card. And there was all the, on the radio from Polish government, are all the people who are able carry things and mobilization cards to move east.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were mobs. You saw sometime we are in the movies or this, in Korea, Vietnam, they go in. So my father says to me, listen. You go with me. We go to the east. So I went with my father. And we went out, and all of a sudden, find out the Russia had come in. We didn't know Russia is coming from the east.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the town named Chelm. And I went to a barber to shave myself, because you know, I must stop, time I clean myself. And all of a sudden, I hear the bombs dropping in the middle of market and outside. Tanks are coming, which we didn't know. Nobody could recognize. They were Russian. We didn't know Russian moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed over there a few days, then went to the north, which I stay over there three weeks. And I got enough of the Russian and went back to-- my mother was with my brothers in Krakow alone. So I told my father, listen, I'm going back. I can't take this Russian business. They got everything, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I smuggled myself to Krakow. Actually, my mother was very surprised that my father didn't came back. So my younger brother heard this. He says, go on in. In three days, he brought his father back to Krakow. So we were all again together until came times to clean Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear what was happening in Germany at the time of the outbreak of the war? Did you hear what the Germans intended doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this nobody believed, anybody. Because we believed in the Polish army. They're going to knock the Germans in two days. In the meantime, the German, you know, finish with Poland two weeks. Polish army were fighting them on horses. And the German had tanks and planes. And we had in the morning, five o'clock in the morning, in Krakow once, September 1, when they're dropping bombs. We were sure this is exercise, Polish exercise. 12 o'clock, the president came on the radio and says, war is over. That's all we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't sort of get a feeling after Kristallnacht the year before that something was not good?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one thing. There's one thing with human people. If doesn't affect you, you don't care. You tell me what's go on in Africa, [INAUDIBLE], did we care? We don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there talk in your family about leaving or taking care of yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Because where we go leave? Where? Where we going go?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to a Jewish youth organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I could go after graduation from this gymnasium to Israel, study, because we got the diploma, which a few guys went and became famous over there. And I somehow-- never came to my mind to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after you returned to Krakow and your family got together? What happened with the Nazis? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to Nazis?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened to them in your town? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they occupied this. You know, they gave-- every few days, a few weeks, we got new orders. You have to wear the Star of David on your arm, register yourself. And then they went by the profession. And one day, they came. They said, listen, you have to leave Krakow. Because the government, which was Frank, has to made capital city of Polish government, so you have to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first contact with the Nazis? When did you first come in contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, there was a curfew , nine o'clock. And I used to go over there after nine o'clock without the things. For the Jews was a curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing I remember, I met on the corner a soldier who came to me. He was a little drunk. And he starts, Heil Hitler. I looked at him and says, tell me, why we are fighting each other? I got nothing against you. You got nothing against me. So I says, listen, what are you asking me? Ask the guy who running the war. Why don't you go with me to the casino, which is right on corner, we will drink. And in the meantime, I see people from the other houses watching me who know me. They say, oh, he's finished because he hasn't got the armband on him. So I says, I tell you one thing. I'm running to my mother. She's very sick. But I tell you one thing. Meet me tomorrow here, and I will very gladly go with you. I think he's still waiting for me. So did you wear a star or you didn't wear a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not always, not always. Somehow, I got away with this. Because like I say, I used to go in a trolley car, which was only for the German in front. And the less people have to go in the back. I went in the front and listened to [INAUDIBLE] when they talk. Somehow, I don't know. Nobody know. And then I went through the war like this, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any non-Jewish friends or neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they respond to what was going on with the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, don't forget. We got workers who work in the office that were not Jewish. Listen, they tried to help, you know, as much they could. But [INAUDIBLE] very, very hard. Very strict. He work hard. You know, we got a partner which was a Gentile guy, very nice. After the war when I came, he took me right away. He gave me quarters, one room. And two days later, the Russian came in and took this apartment from them. So I have to go other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of these non-Jewish people try and help the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yes. Some took advantage, because every time the empty apartment, they went over there and search and rummage. And the Germans knew about this, and they let them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't the Germans who ransacked the apartments?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, the Germans, they gave it to a German guy. He was a nice guy he would let you listen to the radio to the English stations. You know what I mean? But listen, you didn't have time to think. Because every few minutes with different orders, different thing, different decisions you have to make.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you earning your living during this time? How were you getting money, and your family, to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't earn my living, because we lived from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the savings, you know, what we got. And that's where we live. We got a few gold pieces, jewelry, little by little, start selling. We got the apartment, which Persian carpets, all kind of things. So that's where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get produce? Was it easy to trade the stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, always customer. During the war, are you kidding? You got the junk, you can sell, black market. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't forget, in Poland, you always try to have some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dollars, you know, and insurance or something, in case, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, with this going on, did you try and leave the area? Did you think of moving to Western Europe, or out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: How could you? No. I tell you, we were good off, I mean well-off, nice living, nice family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was well-- a nice guy. I got a lot of friends, never came to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you going leave? You got a family here. You got the roots. Where are you going to go? To France or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Czechoslovakia? It wasn't easy to leave. But were the Nazis doing anything to the people in Krakow at the time?

words.text: Where are you going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, listen, they were all the time some kind searching in houses. They came and they started beating up people, killing people once in awhile, with the help of the Polacks. They helped them. And it wasn't pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they decide to make-- was there a ghetto there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left Krakow when we had to leave Krakow. So we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that you have to leave Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this governor Frank decided he wants to have a capital Krakow city. And he gave an order. He can keep only 10,000 people, like tailors, shoemakers, painters, or something like this. You know what I mean? And that's what made us to leave, too. Because we couldn't prove are we tailors or something like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we left the town near Tarnow, which was a small town, a little town where my mother spent her youth. And--
